---
title: "A Finite-M Spectral Correction for Likelihood-Based Inference under Multiple Imputation"
subtitle: "The spectral completion of the Meng–Rubin (1992) / Chan–Meng (2022) program"
author:
  - name: Marcus Waldman
    orcid: 0000-0000-0000-0000
    affiliation: University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus
    role: Human prompter (ORCID-verified)
    email: marcus.waldman@cuanschutz.edu
  - name: Claude (Opus 4.7, 1M context)
    affiliation: Anthropic
    role: AI co-author (derivation and exposition)
date: today
abstract: |
  Multiple imputation (MI) inference combines complete-data analyses across
  imputed datasets to handle missing data. For likelihood-ratio-style hypothesis
  tests, existing combining rules — Meng & Rubin's (1992) D-statistics and
  Chan & Meng's (2022) refinements — calibrate the null distribution using a
  scalar fraction-of-missing-information (FMI) under an Equal-FMI (EFMI)
  assumption. We derive a finite-M moment correction that drops EFMI: an
  obs/mis decomposition of the per-imputation test statistic and a generalized
  symmetric eigendecomposition of a re-based RIV operator yield a canonical
  Karhunen–Loève form. The implied scaled-shifted correction matches both
  moments of the complete-data reference distribution exactly under regularity
  conditions, reduces to Chan & Meng (2022) under EFMI as a rank-one special
  case, and at matched level dominates Chan & Meng in asymptotic power
  whenever the missing-information spectrum is anisotropic. The contribution is
  the spectral completion of a 30-year program: Meng–Rubin (1992) corrected
  the scalar mean; we correct the full multivariate variance at finite M.
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Provenance {.unnumbered}

This manuscript is targeted at [JAIGP](https://jaigp.org) (Journal for AI Generated Papers), which requires transparent attribution of AI involvement.

**Authorship model.** The derivation in this manuscript was developed in collaboration between Marcus Waldman (ORCID-verified human prompter) and Claude (Anthropic, Opus 4.7). Waldman directed the research questions, validated the empirical implications, and made all scoping decisions. The mathematical derivations, expository structure, and proof sketches were generated by Claude in iterative dialogue and then audited by Waldman.

**Lineage and source material.** The technical core builds on a derivation developed in the companion repository [`MI-IC`](https://github.com/marcus-waldman/MI-IC) (private), specifically:

- `claude/derivations/mi_deviance_bias_derivation_v4.qmd` §12 (Theta-subset-Phi closure, v4.4) and §13 (finite-M variance via generalized eigendecomposition, v4.5).

That source document develops the corrections in a structural-equation-modeling (SEM) frame. The present manuscript reframes the same machinery for general likelihood-based inference, omitting SEM-specific scaffolding (CFI/TLI/RMSEA, lavaan, saturated SEM imputation).

**Companion paper.** A separate methodology paper, targeting *Structural Equation Modeling* or *Psychological Methods*, applies the present results to SEM goodness-of-fit testing. That paper cites this one for the derivation; this paper does not cite it back.

**Development log.** Initial conceptual structure articulated in conversation on 2026-05-22. Source v4.5 derivation completed in MI-IC on 2026-04-28 to 2026-04-29 (git history available in the source repository).

---

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Stub.** Motivate the problem: MI is the default for missing-data inference in applied statistics; likelihood-ratio testing under MI has open calibration problems at finite M. Frame the contribution as the spectral completion of M–R / C–M. Preview the three results (exact level, asymptotic envelope, power dominance under anisotropic FMI).
:::

# Setup: Congenial Multiple Imputation and the Q-function {#sec-setup}

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Stub.** Notation: $X = (X_\text{obs}, X_\text{mis})$, likelihood $f(X \mid \psi)$, MI completions $X^{(\ell)}$ for $\ell = 1, \ldots, M$. Congenial imputation per Meng (1994). Q-function $\bar Q(\psi) = M^{-1} \sum_\ell \ell_\text{com}(\psi; X^{(\ell)})$. Complete-data target: $\ell_\text{com}(\hat\theta_\text{com})$.
:::

# Bias Decomposition of the MI Deviance {#sec-bias}

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Stub (Claim 1).** Derive the +½ tr(RIV) bias of $\bar Q(\hat\theta_\text{obs})$ relative to $\ell_\text{com}(\hat\theta_\text{com})$ under congenial MI, decomposed as:

- Term 1 — Imputation bias: $+\text{tr}(\text{RIV})$.
- Term 2 — Estimation mismatch: $-\tfrac{1}{2}\text{tr}(\text{RIV})$.

Source: MI-IC v4.5 §1–§9 (reframe from SEM to general likelihood).
:::

# Obs/Mis Decomposition of MI Test Statistics {#sec-obsmis}

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Stub.** Per-imputation likelihood-ratio statistic $\chi^2_m = A_m + B_m$ split along the observed-vs-missing data factorization:

- As $M \to \infty$: $A_\infty$ is the FIML observed-data LR statistic; $\text{Var}(A_\infty) \approx \text{Var}(\chi^2_\text{com})$.
- $B_\infty = 2\,\text{KL}(p_\text{imp} \,\Vert\, p_{M_1})$ — the imputation-flexibility KL divergence between the imputation distribution and the analysis model.

Reframe: "an MI test statistic is what FIML would give you, plus a penalty for imputation flexibility."
:::

# Generalized Eigendecomposition and Canonical Karhunen–Loève Form {#sec-eigen}

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Stub (Claim 2 core).** Quadratic Taylor expansion of $\bar B_\infty$. Generalized symmetric eigendecomposition (re-base via Cholesky to make $\mathcal{I}^\text{imp,obs}_{\perp\perp}$ and $\mathcal{I}^\text{imp,mis}_{\perp\perp}$ commute). Canonical form:

$$\bar B_\infty = \sum_{j=1}^{\Delta p} \lambda_j Z_j^2$$

where $Z_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ i.i.d. and $\{\lambda_j\}$ are eigenvalues of $\text{RIV}_\perp = \mathcal{I}^\text{imp,mis}_{\perp\perp}(\mathcal{I}^\text{imp,obs}_{\perp\perp})^{-1}$.

Source: MI-IC v4.5 §13.
:::

# Scaled-Shifted Correction {#sec-correction}

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Stub.** Closed-form variance: $\text{Var}[\chi^2_\text{MI}] = \text{Var}[\chi^2_\text{com}] + 2\sum_j \lambda_j^2 + O(M^{-1})$.

Asparouhov–Muthén-style correction:

$$\chi^2_\text{MI,corr} = a\,\chi^2_\text{MI} + b, \quad a = \sqrt{\frac{2\,\text{df}}{2\,\text{df} + 2\sum_j \lambda_j^2}}, \quad b = \text{df}(1-a)$$

Implementation note (symmetrize $W^{-1}B$ via Cholesky for numerical stability).
:::

# Relationship to Meng–Rubin (1992) and Chan–Meng (2022) {#sec-lineage}

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Stub.** Position as the third entry in the lineage.

| Axis | M–R (1992) | C–M (2022) | This work |
|---|---|---|---|
| Numerator construction | average-then-max | max-stacked-data | inherits C–M |
| Null calibration | scalar $r_L$ | scalar $r_m$ (EFMI) | full spectrum of $\text{RIV}_\perp$ |
| Finite-M variance | none | asymptotic in $m$ | finite-$m$, exact under anisotropy |

Show that this work reduces to C–M when the eigenvalue spectrum is isotropic (rank-one collapse: all $\lambda_j$ equal $\Rightarrow$ scaled-shifted correction collapses to scalar mean shift, i.e., C–M's $F$-distribution form).
:::

# Power Theorem {#sec-power}

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Stub (the headline theorem).** Under congenial MI and standard regularity conditions, the scaled-shifted MI test (with C–M-stacked $\hat d_L$ as numerator + spectral calibration):

- (a) Achieves nominal level $\alpha$ exactly in the asymptotic-$N$, fixed-$M$ regime.
- (b) Is asymptotically equivalent to the complete-data LR test in non-centrality structure.
- (c) At matched (size-corrected) level $\alpha$, has asymptotic power $\geq$ Chan–Meng's $\hat D_L$, with strict inequality whenever the $\text{RIV}_\perp$ eigenvalue spectrum is anisotropic.

Proof of (c) goes through Cauchy–Schwarz (Σ$\lambda_j^2 \geq (Σ\lambda_j)^2 / \text{df}$, equality iff isotropic) and Le Cam's third lemma under contiguous alternatives.
:::

# Worked Example: Anisotropic Missing Information in a Multivariate Normal Mean Problem {#sec-example}

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Stub.** A *generic* (non-SEM) example demonstrating the spectral correction's value:

- Multivariate normal mean problem with structured missingness inducing anisotropic $\text{RIV}_\perp$.
- Compare Type I error and power: M–R D_3, C–M $\hat D_L$ (EFMI), this work.
- Show level inflation under EFMI when spectrum is anisotropic; show power dominance at matched level.

Keep this minimal — JAIGP doesn't need a sprawling simulation study. One clean illustrative scenario suffices.
:::

# Discussion {#sec-discussion}

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Stub.** Scope: congenial MI. Open work: uncongenial MI (e.g., PMM) needs separate treatment — see companion MI-IC paper. Implications for software (mice, mitml) and applied practice (when EFMI is plausibly violated). Computational cost of the spectral correction (eigendecomposition of $W^{-1}B$).
:::

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**To populate (`references.bib`):**

- Rubin (1987). *Multiple Imputation for Nonresponse in Surveys.*
- Meng & Rubin (1992). Performing likelihood ratio tests with multiply-imputed data sets. *Biometrika.*
- Meng (1994). Multiple-imputation inferences with uncongenial sources of input. *Statistical Science.*
- Cavanaugh & Shumway (2001). A bootstrap variant of AIC for state-space model selection. *Statistica Sinica.* (and the AICcd reference)
- Chan & Meng (2022). Multiple improvements of multiple imputation likelihood ratio tests. *Statistica Sinica* 32(3): 1489–1514.
- Xie & Meng (2017). Dissecting multiple imputation from a multi-phase inference perspective. *Statistica Sinica.*
- Asparouhov & Muthén — scaled chi-square / mean-and-variance adjustment references.
:::
